articles.title: Cosmic Mystery: Is This a Superkilonova?
articles.summary: Astronomers may have discovered a new type of cosmic explosion called a superkilonova. The event, AT2025ulz, was first detected by gravitational wave observatories on August 18, 2025. It initially looked like a kilonova (a neutron star collision) but later showed supernova features. Scientists think a kilonova may have occurred inside a supernova, hidden from view. If confirmed, it would be the first observation of this phenomenon. The study, led by Mansi Kasliwal, appears in The Astrophysical Journal Letters.

articles.title: AI Auto-Complete: Subtle Mind Shaper?
articles.summary: A new study reveals that AI auto-complete tools can subtly shift people's opinions on social issues without them realizing it. Over 2,500 participants wrote essays with or without AI suggestions that were secretly biased. Those exposed to the biased suggestions moved nearly half a point closer to the AI's stance, even if they didn't use the suggestions. Three-quarters found the AI "reasonable and balanced." The study, published in Science Advances, warns that AI could influence public attitudes and voting behavior. Researchers say warnings don't help much.

articles.title: Building History: Students Recreate ENIAC
articles.summary: Tom Burick, a technology instructor at PS Academy in Arizona, led his neurodivergent students in building a full-scale replica of ENIAC, one of the first programmable electronic computers. The project marked the 80th anniversary of ENIAC's construction. Burick, who has dyscalculia, says his learning difference is a superpower. The replica precisely matches the original 1940s computer. Burick's goal is to ground students' learning in history and leverage their strengths like hyperfocus and precision.
